Document [5] (Title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management) Anti-IL-6 receptor monoclonal antibodies: Interleukin-6 (IL-6) is a proinflammatory cytokine considered the key driver of the hyperinflammatory state associated with COVID-19. Targeting this cytokine with an IL-6 receptor inhibitor could slow down the process of inflammation based on case reports that showed favorable outcomes in patients with severe COVID-19. [108] [109] [110]  The FDA approved three different types of IL-6 receptor inhibitors for various rheumatological conditions (Tocilizumab, Sarilumab) and a rare disorder called Castleman's syndrome (siltuximab). Tocilizumab is an anti-interleukin-6 receptor alpha receptor monoclonal antibody indicated for various rheumatological diseases. The data regarding the use of this agent is mixed. A randomized control trial involving 438 hospitalized patients with severe COVID-19 pneumonia, among which 294 were randomized to receive tocilizumab and 144 to placebo, showed that tocilizumab did not translate into a significant improvement in clinical status or lower the 28-day mortality compared to placebo. [111] Results from another randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial involving patients with confirmed severe COVID-19 that involved 243 patients randomized to receive tocilizumab or placebo showed that the use of tocilizumab was ineffective in preventing intubation or death rate. [112] The REMAP-CAP and RECOVERY trials (not yet published), two large randomized controlled trials, showed evidence of a mortality benefit in patients exhibiting rapid respiratory decompensation. [113] Sarilumab and Siltuximab are IL-6 receptor antagonists that may potentially have a similar effect on the hyperinflammatory state associated with COVID-19 as tocilizumab. Currently, no known published clinical trials support the use of siltuximab in severe COVID-19. Conversely, a 60-day randomized, double-blind placebo control multinational phase 3 trial that evaluated the clinical efficacy, mortality, and safety of sarilumab in 431 patients showed no significant improvement in clinical status or mortality rate. [114] Another randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study on sarilumab's clinical efficacy and safety in adult patients hospitalized with COVID-19 is ongoing (NCT04315298 ).

Document [16] (Title: Bispecific Antibody Toxicity -- Treatment / Management -- Immunosuppression) In grades 3 to 4 CRS, dexamethasone 8 mg every 8 hours intravenously or orally should be administered for 3 days. Steroids should be tapered over 4 days afterward. An inadequate response to these measures warrants intravenous tocilizumab at a dose of 8 mg/kg. Tocilizumab is an IL-6 antagonist that has produced good responses in severe CRS.

Document [19] (Title: Tocilizumab -- Administration -- Breastfeeding women) Tocilizumab can be used while breastfeeding; no human data exists, but based on drug characteristics, there is no risk of infant harm. There is no human data regarding the effects on milk production.
Document [20] (Title: Tocilizumab -- Continuing Education Activity) This activity examines the wide-ranging applications of tocilizumab, a humanized monoclonal antibody approved by the FDA for managing rheumatoid arthritis (RA), giant cell arteritis (GCA), polyarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis (PJIA), systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis (SJIA), and COVID-19 pneumonia. Document [27] (Title: CART Cell Therapy Toxicity -- Treatment / Management -- CRS and ICANS Refractory to the Above Treatment) If despite the use of tocilizumab and corticosteroids, patients with CRS and ICANS continue to worsen, siltuximab, a monoclonal antibody, binds to  IL-6, thus preventing it from binding to the IL-6 receptors. Document [31] (Title: Type III Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Treatment / Management -- Rheumatoid Arthritis) The treatment of rheumatoid arthritis centers around controlling synovitis, preventing joint damage, relieving pain, and preserving musculoskeletal function. Regular physical activity is imperative, and patients should receive proper instruction from a physical therapist and occupational therapist about specific movements that will increase strength and mobility without worsening joint inflammation. Unless contraindicated, all patients with rheumatoid arthritis should receive the disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) methotrexate  as well as NSAIDs or glucocorticoids as needed for symptomatic relief. Patients who fail methotrexate alone may try triple therapy with methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine, and sulfasalazine or add a tumor necrosis factor (TNF) such as etanercept to methotrexate. If a TNF is inappropriate, then methotrexate plus abatacept, a cytotoxic T-lymphocyte antigen-4, tocilizumab, a humanized IgG1 anti-human IL-6 receptor antibody, leflunomide, or sarilumab, a human monoclonal antibody directed against the membrane-bound and soluble IL-6R, are acceptable alternatives. [43] Methotrexate plus rituximab or a Janus kinase inhibitor, such as tofacitinib, are less commonly used combinations.

Document [17] (Title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 416) Amlexanox, fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system as drugs for obstructive airway diseases. Within this broad category, the ATC hierarchy defines two specialized subgroups: “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” and “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants.” The subgroup “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” is an inverse‑isa of the parent class “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES,” indicating that it is a more specific category under the overall group. The same inverse‑isa relationship exists for the inhalant subgroup, and the inhalant subgroup is also explicitly linked to the parent class by an isa relationship, confirming its status as a direct child of the broader category. Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are members of the systemic subgroup, reflecting that they are administered systemically (e.g., oral, subcutaneous or intravenous routes) for the treatment of conditions such as asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and other obstructive airway disorders. Fenspiride is listed as a member of both the systemic subgroup and the inhalant subgroup, suggesting that it may be used either systemically or as an inhaled preparation in the management of obstructive airway disease. The inclusion of biologic agents such as benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab highlights the role of targeted monoclonal‑antibody therapies in severe asthma and related airway inflammation, while amlexanox’s placement in the systemic subgroup points to its potential off‑label or investigational use for airway disease. This ATC classification framework clarifies the therapeutic positioning of each drug, aids clinicians in selecting appropriate treatment routes, and supports consistent pharmacovigilance and prescribing practices for patients with obstructive airway diseases.

Document [24] (Title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 132) Carglumic acid, crizanlizumab, and dextrose monohydrate are all agents that affect blood or body fluids. Crizanlizumab is further defined as a selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitor and is placed within the therapeutic class of immunological agents. In clinical pharmacology it is regarded as an immunomodulator and also exhibits immunosuppressive activity, reflecting its capacity to alter immune pathways. Carglumic acid shares the broader classification of an agent affecting blood or body fluids, while dextrose monohydrate is likewise categorized in this group. Recognizing these interrelated classifications helps clinicians understand the mechanisms of action and therapeutic contexts of each agent, guiding appropriate patient care decisions.
Document [25] (Title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 446) Indomethacin and piroxikam are identified as members of the class “Antiinflammatory agents, non‑steroidal, ophthalmologic,” which itself is a subclass of “antiinflammatory agents, ophthalmologic.” This classification indicates that both drugs are non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory agents used in ophthalmic applications. Although a separate triple suggests a parent‑child link between “imunomodulátory” and indomethacin, the primary pharmacologic categorization of indomethacin remains that of a non‑steroidal ophthalmologic anti‑inflammatory agent rather than an immunomodulator. Crizanlizumab is placed within the therapeutic class “Immunological Agents [TC]” and is described by several related dispositions: it is an “Immunomodulator (disposition),” a “Selective adhesion molecule inhibitor (product),” and, through inverse‑isa relationships, it is an “imunomodulátory” (immunomodulatory) and “imunosupresiva” (immunosuppressive) agent. These overlapping classifications highlight crizanlizumab’s role in modulating and suppressing immune responses by selectively inhibiting adhesion molecules, making it relevant for diseases that require targeted immunomodulation and immunosuppression. Together, the knowledge links indomethacin and piroxikam to ophthalmic non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory therapy, while crizanlizumab is connected to broader immunological therapeutic strategies as an immunomodulator, immunosuppressant, and selective adhesion molecule inhibitor.
Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 92) The ATC category “Other antivirals in ATC” comprises a diverse group of antiviral agents, including enfuvirtide, maraviroc, maribavir, fostemsavir, ibalizumab, moroxydine, and tiloron. Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1884) Amlexanox, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorization indicates that each of these agents is a systemic therapy used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Although they share the same therapeutic class, the drugs differ in their mechanisms of action: amlexanox is a small‑molecule agent, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target interleukin‑5, and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody directed against thymic stromal lymphopoietin. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their common role in managing severe or refractory obstructive airway disease, while highlighting the variety of pharmacologic strategies available within this therapeutic category.

The ATC category “Other antivirals in ATC” includes a diverse set of antiviral drugs: bulevirtide, maravirok (maraviroc), maribavir, moroxydine, fostemsavir, ibalizumab, and tiloron. Together, these relationships illustrate how drugs are organized by therapeutic ATC categories, linked to underlying chemical families such as lipopeptidy, and further defined by their biologic modality when applicable, providing a comprehensive view of their classification and potential mechanisms of action for patient care.

Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 697) Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” category in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system. This grouping indicates that each of these agents is used systemically to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The list includes several monoclonal antibodies—benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab—that target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, as well as roflumilast, a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that helps control inflammation and bronchoconstriction. Amlexanox, although less commonly used for airway disease, is also placed in this category, reflecting its systemic therapeutic role. Grouping these diverse agents together highlights the range of systemic pharmacologic strategies available for managing obstructive airway diseases and underscores their shared classification despite differing mechanisms of action.
